confidence.disclosure: Ahmad ibn Hanbal's life centres on Baghdad, with a band of younger travels to Iraq, Syria, the Hejaz and Yemen for hadith collection (roughly 795–815). The dramatic dates of the Mihna (the inquisition) are exactly known: the inquisition begins in 833, Ahmad is sent in chains toward Caliph al-Ma'mun at Tarsus the same year, al-Ma'mun dies on the road, the trial in front of al-Mu'tasim follows, and Ahmad is imprisoned and flogged for over two years until 835. The Mihna does not formally end until Caliph al-Mutawakkil reverses the policy in 847. Ahmad dies in Baghdad on 12 Rabi al-Awwal 241 AH (2 August 855). His geographic life is small but his political life is dramatic, so this file is medium-density.

sources: Salih ibn Ahmad ibn Hanbal, Sirat al-Imam Ahmad (his son's memoir)
Hanbal ibn Ishaq, Dhikr Mihnat al-Imam Ahmad
Walter Patton, Ahmad ibn Hanbal and the Mihna (1897)
Nimrod Hurvitz, The Formation of Hanbalism (2002)

journey.event: Born in Baghdad in 164 AH (780 CE) shortly after his parents migrated from Merv in Khurasan. Of pure Arab descent from the Banu Shayban tribe of Bakr ibn Wail. His father, a soldier in the Abbasid army, dies when Ahmad is three years old, leaving the family a small property that yields seventeen dirhams a month.

After completing his elementary studies, begins attending higher study circles in Baghdad at age fourteen. Studies jurisprudence under Abu Yusuf, the chief judge of the Abbasid empire and a famous student of Abu Hanifa.

From this year on focuses entirely on the study of hadith — the reports of the Prophet's words and actions. He chooses hadith over fiqh because, in his words, 'in hadith you sit at the feet of those who saw the Companions face to face'.
Begins his great journeys for hadith collection. Travels first to Kufa to learn from Waki ibn al-Jarrah, whose entire collection he memorises.

Over the next fifteen years, criss-crosses Iraq, Syria, the Hejaz and Yemen in the standard scholarly grand tour for hadith — Basra, Mecca, Medina, Damascus, San'a in Yemen. Performs the hajj five times in his life, twice on foot. Lives so frugally on these trips that his clothes are sometimes too ragged to enter the mosque.

Meets Imam al-Shafi'i in Mecca and studies the principles of jurisprudence with him. The encounter shapes his approach to law for the rest of his life. Shafi'i later says of him: 'I left Baghdad and did not leave behind anyone more pious, more cautious, more learned, or more knowledgeable than Ahmad.'

Settles back in Baghdad permanently. Begins teaching from his small house in the al-Rusafa district. Marries for the first time at the age of forty, having spent his youth in study and travel. Lives by the small rental income from his father's property and refuses gifts from any official.

By age forty he is the leading hadith authority in Baghdad. People come from across the empire to study with him. Compiles his vast Musnad — over thirty thousand hadiths organised by the companion who narrated them — drawing on the material gathered in his travelling years.

Caliph al-Ma'mun proclaims the doctrine that the Qur'an is created — a Mu'tazila position — and orders all scholars to publicly affirm it. The Mihna (the inquisition) begins. Most jurists submit; Ahmad refuses. Ordered along with another scholar, Muhammad ibn Nuh, to be brought in chains to the caliph.

Marched in chains across Mesopotamia toward Tarsus in southern Anatolia, where Caliph al-Ma'mun is encamped on a campaign against the Byzantines. Halfway there the news arrives that the caliph has died. Ibn Nuh dies on the road back. Ahmad is brought back to Baghdad to face al-Ma'mun's brother and successor al-Mu'tasim.

Tried over three days in the caliph's palace. Refuses to give any answer not grounded in Qur'an and hadith. On the third day, the caliph orders him flogged in front of the gathered scholars. Ahmad faints under the lashes but never recants. Imprisoned.

Released from prison but kept under house arrest in Baghdad. Forbidden to teach publicly. The persecution continues under Caliph al-Wathiq. For more than a decade Ahmad lives quietly in his small house, teaching only his sons and a few close students in private. Refuses to attend the Friday prayer because he will not pray behind the appointed inquisitors.

Caliph al-Mutawakkil comes to power and reverses the Mihna policy. The doctrine of the created Qur'an is publicly repudiated. Ahmad is honoured throughout the empire as the man who broke the inquisition. He is now over sixty-five and the central figure of Sunni traditionalism.

Caliph al-Mutawakkil summons him to the new Abbasid capital of Samarra to honour him in person and offer him gifts and a teaching post at the palace. Ahmad accepts the visit but refuses every gift, every salary, and every appointment. He returns to his house in Baghdad. He even refuses to allow his own son and uncle to visit him after they take posts at court.
Final illness in his house in Baghdad. His son Salih is at his bedside, reading to him.

Dies on Friday 12 Rabi al-Awwal 241 AH (2 August 855), aged seventy-five. Buried after the afternoon prayer in al-Rusafa. Historians report his funeral as one of the largest gatherings in Baghdad's history — hundreds of thousands of mourners. The mosque around his grave still stands.
